<Summary source="https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/technology/tech-news/us-governments-transition-to-chatgpt-from-anthropic-starts-read-memo/articleshow/128963069.cms">
The following is a summary of the *Times of India* report (via Reuters) regarding the U.S. government’s transition away from Anthropic technology as of March 3, 2026.

### 1. Facts, Statistics, and Objective Measurements
*   **Transition to OpenAI:** Several U.S. federal departments have begun terminating the use of Anthropic’s "Claude" platform and are shifting to OpenAI’s technology.
*   **State Department Memo:** An internal memo reviewed by Reuters confirms the State Department is switching the AI model powering its internal chatbot, "State Chat," from Anthropic to OpenAI’s **GPT-4.1**.
*   **Agency Terminations:** 
    *   The **U.S. Treasury Department** is terminating all use of Anthropic products.
    *   The **Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA)**, along with **Fannie Mae** and **Freddie Mac**, are ending their use of Anthropic tools.
*   **Timeline:** While some agencies are moving immediately, others are expected to phase out the technology over a **six-month period**, as per the presidential directive.
*   **Supply Chain Designation:** The Pentagon has announced its intention to declare Anthropic a "supply-chain risk to national security," a move that would prohibit U.S. defense contractors from using the company's tools.

### 2. Opinions from Reliable and Named Sources
*   **Scott Bessent (U.S. Treasury Secretary):** Stated via X that the Treasury Department is terminating all use of Anthropic products.
*   **William Pulte (Director of the FHFA):** Confirmed that his agency and associated mortgage giants are ending their use of Anthropic tools.
*   **President Donald Trump:** Characterized Anthropic as a "Radical Left" and "woke" company. He alleged the company made a "disastrous mistake" by attempting to "strong-arm" the Department of War into following Terms of Service that he claims conflict with the Constitution. He stated that Anthropic’s refusal to allow certain uses of its AI puts "American lives at risk."
*   **Department of War/Pentagon:** The article notes the standoff was triggered by Anthropic’s refusal to allow its models to be used for **mass domestic surveillance** or **fully autonomous weapons systems**.

### 3. Potentially Useful Opinions from Less Reliable/Not-Named Sources
*   **Reuters/Internal Memo:** The report cites an internal State Department memo regarding the switch to GPT-4.1; while the memo itself is a primary source, the specific "further details" mentioned in the memo have not yet been shared publicly.
*   **General Context:** The article attributes the shift to a "dramatic standoff" over AI guardrails, framing the conflict as a choice between corporate Terms of Service and national security requirements as defined by the current administration.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/27/pentagon-moves-to-designate-anthropic-as-a-supply-chain-risk/">
This summary focuses on the key facts and statements from the *TechCrunch* article regarding the designation of Anthropic as a supply chain risk.

### 1. Facts, Statistics, and Objective Measurements
*   **Executive Action:** President Trump directed all federal agencies to cease using Anthropic products via a Truth Social post on February 27, 2026.
*   **Phase-out Period:** The President’s directive includes a six-month phase-out period for departments currently using Anthropic products.
*   **Supply Chain Designation:** Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth officially directed the Department of War to designate Anthropic a "Supply-Chain Risk to National Security."
*   **Scope of Restriction:** The designation mandates that no contractor, supplier, or partner doing business with the U.S. military may conduct any commercial activity with Anthropic, effective immediately.
*   **Contractual Context:** Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google had all received contract awards from the U.S. Defense Department in July 2025.
*   **Competitor Movement:** Following the order against Anthropic, OpenAI announced a new deal with the Pentagon. Reports indicate OpenAI began meetings regarding this tie-up on Wednesday, February 25, 2026.

### 2. Opinions from Reliable and Named Sources
*   **Pete Hegseth (Secretary of Defense):** Characterized Anthropic’s terms of service as "unduly restrictive." He stated the transition period (no more than six months) is intended to allow for a "seamless transition to a better and more patriotic service."
*   **Dario Amodei (CEO, Anthropic):** Stated the company’s "strong preference" is to serve the Department of War with two specific safeguards: no use for mass domestic surveillance and no use for fully autonomous weapons. He confirmed Anthropic will challenge the supply chain risk designation in court.
*   **Sam Altman (CEO, OpenAI):** Per a memo reported by the *BBC*, Altman claimed to share Anthropic’s "red lines" regarding domestic surveillance and autonomous offensive weapons, asserting that OpenAI’s new deal preserves these core principles.
*   **Ilya Sutskever (Co-founder, Safe Superintelligence/OpenAI):** Stated on X (formerly Twitter) that it is "extremely good" that Anthropic did not back down and "significant" that OpenAI initially took a similar stance.

### 3. Potentially Useful Opinions from Less Reliable/Not-Named Sources
*   **The New York Times (Reporting):** Cited that OpenAI and the government began meetings about a potential deal on Wednesday of the same week the Anthropic dispute peaked.
*   **Google Employees:** Some employees have expressed support for Anthropic’s position, though Google and Alphabet leadership have not issued an official comment.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.bnnbloomberg.ca/business/2026/03/06/anthropic-vows-court-fight-in-pentagon-row/">
The following is a summary of the key facts and opinions from the March 6, 2026, *BNN Bloomberg* article regarding the legal dispute between Anthropic and the U.S. Department of War.

### 1. Facts, Statistics, and Objective Measurements
*   **Formal Designation:** Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei confirmed the company received a letter from the Department of War officially designating Anthropic and its products (including the Claude AI model) as a **supply chain risk**.
*   **Historical Precedent:** This is the first time a U.S.-based company has been publicly given this designation; previously, the label has been applied to entities from foreign adversaries, such as Huawei.
*   **Compliance Requirements:** Defense vendors and contractors are now required to certify that they do not use Anthropic’s models specifically for their work with the Pentagon.
*   **Political Contributions:** The article notes that OpenAI President Greg Brockman has donated **$25 million** to Donald Trump.
*   **Competitor Status:** OpenAI initially moved to replace Anthropic in its military contracts, though CEO Sam Altman later described the deal as "sloppy" and stated it is being revised due to internal staff discomfort.

### 2. Opinions from Reliable and Named Sources
*   **Dario Amodei (CEO, Anthropic):**
    *   States the company has **"no choice"** but to challenge the designation in court, disputing its legal basis.
    *   Argues the practical scope is **narrower than suggested**: it applies only to the use of Claude as a "direct part" of Department of War contracts, not to all activities of customers who happen to hold such contracts.
    *   Contends that the relevant statute requires the government to use the **"least restrictive means necessary"** and is intended to protect the government rather than punish suppliers.
    *   Initially claimed in a leaked memo that the move was **politically motivated** due to a lack of campaign donations, though he later characterized this as an "out-of-date assessment" written under duress.
*   **Microsoft (Anthropic Partner):**
    *   Legal counsel concluded that Anthropic products **can remain available** to Microsoft customers other than the Department of War, supporting Amodei’s "narrow" interpretation of the ruling.
*   **Department of War / Pete Hegseth:**
    *   Maintains that contracted suppliers **cannot dictate terms** on how their products are used (referring to Anthropic’s refusal to allow use for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons).
*   **Sam Altman (CEO, OpenAI):**
    *   Admitted the initial move to take over Anthropic’s military contract was **"sloppy"** and is currently being revised.

### 3. Potentially Useful Opinions from Less Reliable/Unnamed Sources
*   **Internal Anthropic Memo (Leaked):** Suggested the administration’s actions were a result of Anthropic not donating to the Trump campaign while competitors did. (Note: While the memo is a primary document, Amodei has since distanced himself from its conclusions, calling it an "out-of-date assessment.")
</Summary>


<Summary source="https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c5y4jpg922qo">
This summary focuses on the key facts and legal developments from the provided BBC article regarding Anthropic’s copyright settlement, which may inform the company's legal standing and "ethical" reputation leading into the 2026 forecast period.

### 1. Facts, Statistics, and Objective Measurements
*   **Settlement Amount:** Anthropic agreed to pay **$1.5 billion** to settle a class-action lawsuit filed by authors. This is cited as the largest publicly reported copyright recovery in history.
*   **Legal Context:** The settlement follows a June 2025 ruling by US District Judge William Alsup. While the judge found that using books to train AI was "exceedingly transformative" and not a violation of copyright law, he ordered Anthropic to stand trial for the use of **pirated material**.
*   **Scale of Infringement:** The court noted that Anthropic held more than **seven million pirated books** in a central library. The company faced potential statutory damages of up to $150,000 per copyrighted work.
*   **Corporate Backing:** The article notes that Anthropic is backed by major tech entities **Amazon** and **Alphabet** (Google’s parent company).
*   **Timeline:** The settlement was announced on September 5, 2025, narrowly avoiding a trial that had been scheduled for December 2025.

### 2. Opinions from Reliable and Named Sources
*   **Aparna Sridhar (Deputy General Counsel, Anthropic):** Stated the settlement resolves "legacy claims" and reaffirmed the company's commitment to developing "safe AI systems" and acting as an ethical alternative to competitors.
*   **Judge William Alsup (US District Judge):** Ruled that the general use of books for AI training is "exceedingly transformative" under US law, but distinguished this from the acquisition of data from pirate websites.
*   **Justin Nelson (Plaintiffs' Lawyer):** Characterized the settlement as a "first of its kind" precedent that sends a "powerful message" that using pirate websites to train AI is legally "wrong."
*   **Alex Yang (Professor, London Business School):** Opined that AI development requires "fresh training data from human beings" and argued that if AI-created content is granted copyright, there must be stronger mechanisms to compensate human creators.

### 3. Potentially Useful Opinions from Less Reliable/Unnamed Sources
*   **Plaintiffs' Claims:** The authors (including Andrea Bartz, Charles Graeber, and Kirk Wallace Johnson) accused Anthropic of "stealing" their work to build a multi-billion dollar business.
*   **General Industry Sentiment:** The article notes that Anthropic has "long pitched itself as the ethical alternative" among AI competitors like OpenAI and Meta, a reputation this settlement and the underlying use of pirated material may complicate.
</Summary>

Query: Anthropic exclusion judicial stay request
Here are the relevant news articles:

**Anthropic CEO to Challenge 'Supply Chain Risk' Designation by US Department of War in Court**
On March 5 (local time), Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei announced via his official blog that he received a formal letter designating Anthropic as a 'supply chain risk' by the US Department of War (DoW). Amodei stated he will contest this designation in court, arguing it is legally unjustified, while clarifying that the scope is narrow, affecting only AI use directly involved in DoW contractual work, and not general customers or other commercial transactions. The conflict originated when the DoW demanded AI companies remove safety safeguards and agree to 'all lawful uses' of AI by the military; Anthropic agreed to cooperate but requested exceptions for 'large-scale domestic surveillance' and 'fully autonomous weapons,' citing significant dangers. Negotiations stalled, leading Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to warn that the company would be designated as a supply chain risk, implying effective exclusion from trade. During this standoff, Anthropic's competitor, OpenAI, announced a contract to deploy its AI models on the DoW's classified network. Media reports revealed an internal memo from Amodei harshly criticizing OpenAI, calling their announcement 'false' and claiming their software controls were mostly 'show' in a military context. Amodei apologized on his March 5 blog post, explaining the memo was written on a difficult day with overlapping presidential posts and official designations and did not reflect his current cautious views. Amodei noted he has been having productive dialogues with the DoW over the past few days to find a way to continue providing technology while adhering to the two exception conditions under the shared goal of advancing US national security, or to find a path for smooth transition to other companies if agreement is not reached. The company also promised to continue providing models and engineering support at minimal cost during the transition period, stating they should not abruptly deprive frontline soldiers of important tools, and intends to maintain a cooperative stance with the military until legally cut off from access or banned from service provision.
Original language: ja
Publish date: March 06, 2026 10:52 PM
Source:[ITmedia](https://www.itmedia.co.jp/news/articles/2603/07/news030.html)

**Google joins Microsoft in telling users Anthropic is still available outside defense projects**
Google and Microsoft have announced they will continue offering Anthropic's artificial intelligence technology to their clients, excluding defense-related work, following the U.S. Department of Defense's (DOD) designation of Anthropic as a 'supply chain risk.' Google CEO Sundar Pichai's company confirmed this stance on Friday, with a spokesperson stating, 'We understand that the Determination does not preclude us from working with Anthropic on non-defense related projects, and their products remain available through our platforms, like Google Cloud.' This move follows a similar statement from Microsoft, which noted that 'Anthropic products, including Claude, can remain available to our customers -- other than the Department of War.' The DOD's decision came after Anthropic refused to agree to requested terms of use, prompting President Donald Trump to instruct federal agencies to stop using the company's technology, while Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth stated that work with Anthropic would wind down over six months. Despite this, CNBC has confirmed that Anthropic models were used by the U.S. in its latest attack on Iran. Google, a significant financial backer with a $2 billion previous stake and an additional $1 billion investment agreed to in January 2025, allows access to Anthropic's Claude models via the Vertex AI platform and provides up to 1 million custom tensor processing units (TPUs) for training. In response to the designation, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei stated on Thursday that his company has 'no choice' but to challenge the ruling in court. Amazon, the leader in public cloud, has not yet issued a comment on the matter.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 06, 2026 06:25 PM
Source:[CNBC](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/06/google-says-anthropic-remains-available-outside-of-defense-projects.html)

**Anthropic vows court fight in Pentagon row**
Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei announced on Thursday, March 6, 2026, that the company will challenge the Pentagon's designation of Anthropic and its Claude AI model as a 'supply chain risk' to US national security in court. This marks the first time a US company has received such a label, which is typically reserved for foreign adversaries like Huawei. According to a letter from the Department of War (the Trump administration's preferred name for the Department of Defense), the designation requires defense vendors to certify they do not use Anthropic's models in Pentagon contracts. However, Amodei argued in a blog post that the ruling's scope is narrow, applying only to direct contracts with the Department of War, not all customers. Microsoft, a major partner, confirmed this interpretation, stating Anthropic's products remain available to customers outside the Department of War. The dispute originated after Anthropic refused Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth's request to use its technology for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons. Amodei also addressed a leaked internal memo where he claimed the Trump administration targeted the company due to Anthropic's lack of political donations compared to OpenAI's Greg Brockman, who donated $25 million to Trump; Amodei later apologized for the memo, calling it an 'out-of-date assessment' written under duress. OpenAI had initially attempted to replace Anthropic in the military contract, but CEO Sam Altman later described the deal as 'sloppy' and sought revisions.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 06, 2026 02:44 PM
Source:[AFP](http://doc.afp.com/A29K2T3)

**US Defense Department Excludes Domestic AI Firm Anthropic Over Supply Chain Risks**
On March 5 (local time), the US Department of Defense (referred to as the 'War Department' in the text) officially notified AI company Anthropic that it has been designated a 'supply chain risk' entity and will be excluded from transactions with partners cooperating with the Department, effective immediately. This marks the first time such a measure has been applied to a domestic company rather than an adversary nation. According to a report by The Wall Street Journal (WSJ), a senior Department official stated the decision relates to the 'fundamental principle that the military must be able to use technology for all legitimate purposes,' adding that the military 'will not tolerate situations where suppliers interfere with the chain of command or put combatants at risk by restricting the legitimate use of core technologies.' Under the designation, not only the Department but also defense contractors contracting with it must certify that Anthropic products are not used in services provided to the military. The conflict arose previously as Anthropic had requested guarantees that its AI models would not be used for large-scale domestic surveillance or autonomous weapon systems. The article notes the report was published on March 6, 2026, at 08:34 by reporter Lee Hye-in for Hankyung.com.

**US Defense Department Designates Anthropic a Supply Chain Risk Amid AI Dispute**
On Thursday evening, the US Department of Defense designated Anthropic, a leading artificial intelligence (AI) company, as a 'supply chain risk,' restricting its ability to collaborate with defense contractors. Dario Amodei, CEO of Anthropic, stated the company intends to challenge this designation in court. The tension stems from weeks of strained relations: US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth had demanded Anthropic make its AI systems available for any military use, including mass surveillance and autonomous weapons, which Anthropic sought to prevent. The Pentagon issued an ultimatum, but negotiations were abruptly halted by Donald Trump just before the deadline, ordering all federal agencies to immediately cease using Anthropic's tools. Consequently, Hegseth threatened and subsequently executed the exclusion of Anthropic from Pentagon supplies. Currently, Claude, Anthropic's chatbot, is widely used by the US military. This conflict highlights Anthropic's strategy to distinguish itself from competitors like Meta, Alphabet, and OpenAI by prioritizing user data protection and exercising caution in developing systems that could harm the collective.

**Legally unsound: Anthropic fights back against DoD's AI supply chain exclusion**
Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei has vowed to sue the US Department of Defense (DoD) after the agency designated Anthropic as a 'Supply-Chain Risk to National Security,' a move Amodei described as 'legally unsound.' This designation, issued on March 06, 2026, could block Anthropic from securing contracts with the Pentagon and its contractors. The conflict stems from a disagreement over AI oversight: Amodei established a 'red line' that Anthropic's flagship model, Claude, would not be used for mass surveillance of US citizens or autonomous killer robots, whereas the DoD sought 'blanket access' for 'all lawful purposes.' Amodei clarified that the designation applies only to 'use of Claude by customers as a direct part of contracts with the Department of War,' arguing that existing law requires the 'least restrictive means necessary' and cannot limit unrelated business relationships. The situation escalated following a leaked internal memo where Amodei dismissed OpenAI's Pentagon ties as 'safety theatre.' In response, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman warned against firms ditching democratic principles and admitted OpenAI's subsequent DoD pact was 'opportunistic and sloppy' but necessary to prevent disaster. The conflict was further inflamed by Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth, who publicly labeled Anthropic a risk on X, and President Donald Trump, who ordered federal agencies to halt use of Anthropic's technology. Meanwhile, OpenAI 'swiftly inked' its own DoD pact mere hours after the blacklist. The article notes that this clash highlights the fault lines of the AI arms race: 'ethics versus national security, corporate independence versus government might.'

**Executive Branch Targets Anthropic as Supply Chain Risk: Key Considerations for Federal Contractors**
In response to a months-long dispute between the Department of Defense (DoD) and Anthropic regarding usage restrictions for its AI platform, Claude, President Trump directed all U.S. federal agencies via a social media post to 'IMMEDIATELY CEASE all use of Anthropic's technology' with a 'six month phase out period for agencies,' including the DoD. In conjunction with this directive, Secretary of War Pete Hegseth ordered the DoD to designate Anthropic a 'Supply-Chain Risk to National Security,' prohibiting contractors, suppliers, or partners from conducting 'any commercial activity with Anthropic' effective immediately, while allowing Anthropic to continue services for 'no more than six months' to facilitate a transition. These announcements have raised significant legal and operational questions within the federal contracting community. The directives appear to rely on two statutory authorities, though it remains unclear whether the required procedural requirements, scope limitations, and interagency processes have been formally invoked or completed. To date, no formal Federal Acquisition Security Council (FASCSA) exclusion order or recommendation has been publicly issued, and it remains unclear whether a government-wide exclusion order will be pursued or if agency-specific steps are required before prohibitions become contractually enforceable. Anthropic has publicly indicated it intends to challenge any formal exclusion action in court, noting that future judicial proceedings could affect the timing, scope, or enforceability of any exclusion measures. As of March 05, 2026, no formal regulatory action or contractually binding exclusion has been issued requiring contractors to discontinue use, leaving the environment fluid for federal contractors to prepare contingency plans.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 05, 2026 10:40 PM
Source:[The National Law Review - A Free To Use Nationwide Database of Legal Publications](https://natlawreview.com/article/executive-branch-targets-anthropic-supply-chain-risk-key-considerations-federal)

**OpenAI Users Flee ChatGPT After Defense Deal; Anthropic Gains 51% in Downloads**
Following a contract signed by OpenAI with the U.S. Department of Defense in late February 2026, users have abandoned ChatGPT in large numbers, while competitor Anthropic has seen significant growth. According to data from Sensor Tower, ChatGPT app uninstallations quadrupled immediately after the news of the agreement became public. Concurrently, Anthropic's AI app, Claude, saw downloads increase by 51%, propelling it to the top of the U.S. App Store rankings. The conflict stems from ethical disagreements regarding military AI applications; while OpenAI signed the deal, Anthropic previously refused to relax safety rules to allow for mass surveillance and potentially autonomous weapons systems, which the U.S. Department of Defense reportedly requested. Although Anthropic's technology was integrated into Pentagon systems in 2025, the company now risks exclusion from future security procurement processes. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman publicly addressed the backlash by publishing an internal memo on X, admitting the deal might appear 'opportunistic and poorly managed' to outsiders. Altman explained that the decision was made to avoid a potentially more problematic contract and to maintain influence over usage conditions, while noting that OpenAI shares 'red lines' with Anthropic regarding the current maturity of the technology. In response to criticism, Altman announced plans to clarify the agreement's terms to exclude specific uses, such as by the National Security Agency or for American citizen surveillance, and to impose stricter limits on autonomous weapons, though it remains uncertain if the Department of Defense will accept these restrictions. Social media reactions indicate growing public sensitivity to military AI projects, with users accusing OpenAI of sacrificing ethical principles for political interests, highlighting a strategic crisis of trust for the entire AI industry.
Original language: it
Publish date: March 05, 2026 08:33 PM
Source:[www.Bluewin.ch](https://www.bluewin.ch/it/attualita/diversi/gli-utenti-abbandonano-chatgpt-in-massa-ecco-cosa-ce-dietro-3126086.html)

**WTT Participações Seeks Exclusion of FW Joint Venture from Fictor's Judicial Recovery**
WTT Participações, a renewable energy company based in Goiânia, has petitioned the São Paulo Court to exclude the FW joint venture from the judicial recovery process of the Fictor group, which is facing scrutiny following the November 2025 liquidation of Banco Master over alleged financial frauds. The FW joint venture, established in 2023, is a partnership where Fictor holds a 51% stake and WTT holds 49%, managing a portfolio comprising 13 solar plants totaling 42.85 megawatts (MW) and five thermal projects totaling 426.4 MW. According to the petition filed with the 3rd Bankruptcy and Judicial Recovery Court of the São Paulo Court of Justice (TJ-SP), WTT argues that including the joint venture in Fictor's recovery violates the Brazilian Corporations Law (6.404/1976) and the joint venture's bylaws, which require unanimous shareholder consent for such decisions. Sources told Valor Econômico that WTT was surprised by the request, calling it 'unfounded, illegal, and illegitimate.' Additionally, WTT has requested a preliminary injunction to suspend Fictor's political rights within FW and to exclude Fictor from the partnership entirely. This move follows allegations that Fictor breached a shareholders' agreement by failing to deliver the required R$ 1 billion capital contribution, having only deposited R$ 100 million. While the 13 solar plants are located in four states (SP, AM, GO, and RJ) with five currently operational, WTT intends to retain management of the assets if Fictor is excluded, rather than selling them. Fictor did not respond to requests for comment by the time of publication.

**Anthropic Resumes Pentagon AI Talks After Refusing Unrestricted Military Use**
Anthropic, a leading artificial intelligence company, has resumed negotiations with the U.S. Department of Defense (Pentagon) to secure a contract for its AI systems, following a suspension of talks last week. The negotiations were abruptly halted when Anthropic refused Pentagon demands to allow unrestricted military use of its technology, specifically opposing deployment in mass surveillance programs and autonomous weapons systems, according to a report by the Financial Times. During the suspension, President Trump ordered all federal agencies to immediately cease using Anthropic's tools, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth threatened to ban Anthropic from the Pentagon's supply chain. Currently, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei is negotiating with Under Secretary of Defense Emil Michael to reach an agreement that prevents Anthropic's exclusion from the supply chain. Meanwhile, the Pentagon had signed a similar agreement with OpenAI, the maker of the ChatGPT chatbot and Anthropic's competitor, shortly after the Anthropic talks broke down; however, that agreement was modified days later following significant public criticism that it lacked clear usage limits, unlike the restrictions Anthropic had previously insisted upon. The report was published by Il Post on March 05, 2026.

**Anthropic and U.S. Defense Department Clash Over AI Dual-Use Dilemma and Self-Regulation Failure**
On March 1, 2026, the U.S. Department of Defense designated AI startup Anthropic as a 'supply chain risk enterprise' and ordered a complete suspension of its technology use within federal agencies, escalating a conflict that highlights the structural limits of the AI industry's 'self-regulation' model. According to industry sources on February 2, the conflict erupted after Anthropic discovered its model 'Claude' was used in a January 2026 operation to capture Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, prompting Anthropic to allege a violation of its ethics policy. Anthropic has maintained ethical guidelines prohibiting the use of its models for large-scale domestic surveillance and lethal autonomous weapons systems (LAWS) without human intervention, whereas the Department of Defense insists AI must be available for 'all lawful purposes' in the interest of national security. As the gap in usage scope remains unresolved, Anthropic faces increasing likelihood of exclusion from the federal procurement system. This dispute intensified as Anthropic effectively withdrew its initial principle of delaying powerful AI system releases until sufficient safety was ensured, signaling a full entry into the high-stakes performance race driven by big tech competition. An industry expert explained that as model capabilities advance, data integration and complex reasoning strengthen, creating 'dual-use' characteristics where the same technology serves as advanced business analysis tools for corporate clients while simultaneously enhancing surveillance precision and military decision-making support. Consequently, industry observers question the long-term viability of Anthropic's strategy, which seeks commercial expansion through technological advancement while restricting military use of the same technology. Following Anthropic's potential exclusion, competitor OpenAI signed a contract with the U.S. Department of Defense for classified network deployment, widening the strategic divide within the AI sector. MIT physicist Max Tegmark, speaking to TechCrunch, interpreted the incident as a structural problem, noting that major AI companies previously refused to support binding legislation, relying instead on self-regulation without a clear legal framework, which left no institutional mechanism to resolve conflicts with state power. Tegmark argued that uncontrolled ultra-advanced AI poses greater national security risks than the speed of competition with China, stating that 'if companies had requested government legislation based on their past safety promises, this situation would not have occurred,' and that relying on self-regulation in a speed race makes it difficult for both companies and governments to maintain control.
Original language: ko
Publish date: March 01, 2026 11:58 PM
Source:[ZDNET 코리아](https://zdnet.co.kr/view/)

**Anthropic Warns of Lawsuit After Pentagon Exclusion, While OpenAI Secures Defense Contract**
Anthropic, the company behind the AI service Claude, has announced plans to sue the Pentagon after being excluded from defense contracts. Pentagon Chief Pete Hegseth cited national security risks, stating that Anthropic poses a 'supply chain risk' due to its refusal to grant the Pentagon unrestricted access to its AI technology for mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons systems. Hegseth declared that no contractor, vendor, or partner could conduct any commercial activity with Anthropic, effective immediately. Anthropic rejected the Pentagon's demands, arguing they violate its AI ethics guidelines. In a statement, Anthropic vowed to challenge the 'supply chain risk' designation in court, asserting, 'No form of intimidation or punishment from the Department of Defense will make us change our stance on domestic mass surveillance or fully autonomous weapons.' The Pentagon's decision followed a directive from former President Donald Trump, who ordered all federal agencies to terminate partnerships with Anthropic. Shortly after, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman announced that OpenAI had secured a contract with the Pentagon to use its AI models in classified cloud-based networks. Altman stated, 'In all our meetings, the Department of Defense has shown great respect for security and a desire to be a partner in achieving the best possible solution.'

**Anthropic Stands Firm Against Pentagon’s AI Weapons Demands**
Anthropic, led by CEO Dario Amodei, has refused the Pentagon's demand to remove safety restrictions from its Claude AI system for unrestricted military use, including autonomous weapons and large-scale domestic surveillance. The Pentagon issued a Friday 5:01 p.m. ultimatum, threatening to exclude Anthropic from defense contracts and classify it as a 'supply chain risk'—a designation typically reserved for adversarial nations. Defense officials, including Secretary Pete Hegseth, have threatened to invoke the Defense Production Act to compel compliance, though legal scholars question its constitutionality. Anthropic rejected revised contract terms delivered Wednesday evening, calling them 'virtually no progress' and noting they allowed protective measures to be disregarded at will. Amodei stated, 'these threats do not change our position: we cannot in good conscience accede to their request,' emphasizing that current AI is not reliable enough for fully autonomous weapons and warning that AI can aggregate innocuous data into invasive personal profiles at scale. While supporting AI in foreign intelligence, Anthropic opposes domestic surveillance. Defense Undersecretary Emil Michael countered that such applications are already prohibited by law and accused Amodei of attempting to 'personally control the US Military.' The company proposed collaborative R&D to improve AI reliability for defense use, but the offer was declined. With a $200 million annual framework agreement at stake and potential exclusion from a 60,000-company defense contractor ecosystem, Anthropic remains steadfast, and the standoff persists as of Thursday evening with the Friday deadline unchanged.

**U.S. Military Demands Unrestricted AI Use, Anthropic Refuses: 'Cannot Compromise Conscience'**
U.S. military officials demanded unrestricted use of Anthropic's AI large model, Claude, but the company's CEO, Dario Amodei, rejected the request, stating, 'These threats will not change our position: we cannot compromise our conscience to accept their demands.' According to the 'Politico' news report, the Pentagon exerted extreme pressure on Anthropic, threatening to label the company a 'supply chain risk'—a designation previously reserved only for foreign adversaries—if it did not comply by February 27, 2026, at 5:01 p.m. U.S. time. Amodei clarified that while Anthropic supports military decision-making by the Department of Defense and has not opposed specific military applications, it refuses to allow two categories of use: mass surveillance within the U.S. and fully autonomous weapons systems that operate without human intervention. He emphasized that these restrictions were never part of their contract with the Pentagon and remain unacceptable today. The Pentagon reportedly insisted on contracts with companies willing to permit AI use in 'any lawful purpose' and to waive safety measures. Amodei warned that failure to uphold these safeguards would result in exclusion from defense contracts and potential enforcement under the Defense Production Act. Despite the standoff, Amodei left room for negotiation, stating that while the Pentagon has the right to choose contractors, Anthropic hopes the military will reconsider, as its technology provides significant value to U.S. forces. The conflict intensified after reports emerged that Claude was used in a U.S. military operation against Venezuela, prompting Anthropic to verify the claim and express concern. A meeting between Amodei and Defense Secretary Hegseth on February 24 failed to resolve the disagreement.

**Extraconcursal Creditors Can Seek Seizure After Stay Period Ends, STJ Rules**
The Brazilian Superior Tribunal de Justiça (STJ) ruled unanimously that, after the 180-day stay period (extendable by another 180 days) under the Law of Judicial Recovery and Bankruptcy, creditors with extraconcursal claims may request enforcement actions such as asset seizure. The 4th Turma of the STJ upheld an appeal by an investment fund against two companies in judicial recovery. A judge in Itajaí, Santa Catarina, had ordered the release of funds in a bank account of the recovering companies, allowing them to use the money freely. The creditor appealed, arguing the court below violated provisions of the Law of Judicial Recovery and Bankruptcy by denying the extraconcursal nature of a credit derived from a fiduciary assignment contract, which had already been confirmed in a final judgment. The state court denied the appeal, stating the funds were essential for the companies’ survival. The STJ, however, ruled that fiduciary-secured credits are expressly excluded from the effects of judicial recovery under consolidated STJ jurisprudence. Justice Isabel Gallotti, the rapporteur, emphasized that the assets in question were not capital but money held as fiduciary collateral, and thus not protected under the principle of preserving the company’s essential assets. She further noted that Law 14.112/2020, which applies immediately to ongoing cases, prohibits judges from blocking the satisfaction of extraconcursal claims once the stay period ends. According to her, 'once the stay period under Article 6º, §4º of Law 11.101/2005 has ended, the judicial recovery court cannot obstruct the enforcement of an extraconcursal credit based on the principle of preserving the company.'

**Is Your Site's Robots.txt Giving Content to AI Models for Free?**
The robots.txt file, a foundational component of the Robots Exclusion Protocol, is evolving from a tool for guiding search engine crawlers to a critical legal instrument in the ongoing battle over AI training data. Historically used to direct bots on which parts of a website to access, robots.txt is now central in lawsuits involving AI companies' data scraping practices. While courts have ruled that robots.txt alone does not qualify as a 'technological measure' under the DMCA’s anti-circumvention provisions—because it is voluntary and not a technical barrier—it can serve as evidence of lack of authorization when combined with other protections. In Ziff Davis v. OpenAI, the court dismissed a DMCA claim based solely on robots.txt, but Reddit’s lawsuit against SerpApi demonstrates a stronger strategy: bypassing robots.txt alongside other technical safeguards (e.g., search engine access controls), thereby creating a more compelling case for circumvention. A second legal theory, breach of contract, is gaining traction, as seen in Reddit v. Anthropic, where Reddit argues that AI companies like Anthropic knowingly violated its terms of service by scraping content instead of using its authorized API. This theory hinges on whether sophisticated AI firms can claim ignorance of terms they had actual notice of. Businesses are advised to audit their robots.txt and terms of service to ensure they actively protect content rather than implicitly granting access. Although robots.txt cannot alone enforce legal liability, it strengthens legal arguments by signaling that no implied license exists and that unauthorized access is intentional.

**Spagnuolo Requests Exclusion of Audio Evidence in Andis Bribery Case**
Diego Spagnuolo, former director of the National Disability Agency (Andis) until mid-August, filed a legal motion before the Federal Court requesting the exclusion of audio recordings that detail a corruption scheme within Andis, seeking the dismissal of his case. His defense attorney, Florencia Plazas, argued that the recordings were obtained through 'covert and hidden' methods, violating fundamental constitutional rights, including the right to privacy and the inviolability of communications. The defense claims the recordings lack legal validity and cannot serve as the basis for a criminal investigation. Spagnuolo aligned his legal strategy with that of the Kovalivker brothers, owners of Suizo Argentina pharmacy, who are under investigation for allegedly paying bribes to Andis officials. The defense also invoked a 'lack of action' claim by the Kovalivkers, arguing that the current case is identical to a previously dismissed one. The case has been transferred to Chamber II of the Federal Court, and the proceedings remain under judicial secrecy. Meanwhile, federal prosecutor Franco Picardi ordered 25 simultaneous raids 15 days ago, seizing phones and electronic devices from pharmacies and private residences. The alleged corruption network, which reportedly involves Karina Milei and Eduardo 'Lule' Menem according to the recordings, continues to destabilize the government.

**Prokurator Generalny Requests Exclusion of Supreme Court Judges Appointed After 2017 from Professional Responsibility Chamber**
The Prokurator Generalny (PG), Waldemar Żurek, has submitted a formal request to exclude six Supreme Court (SN) judges appointed after 2017 from serving in the Izba Odpowiedzialności Zawodowej (Professional Responsibility Chamber) of the Supreme Court. These judges—Tomasz Demendecki, Marek Dobrowolski, Marek Motuk, Marek Siwek, Maria Szczepaniec, and Paweł Wojciechowski—were appointed by the Krajowa Rada Sądownictwa (National Council of the Judiciary) under a structure established during the PiS government. The PG argues that the appointment process was tainted by excessive influence from the legislative and executive branches, undermining judicial independence and the constitutional requirement for an independent court. The PG's position is supported by rulings from European courts, including the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) and the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU), which emphasize that a court can only be considered 'established by law' if its judicial appointments guarantee independence and public trust. The PG highlights that the cumulative damages awarded in 91 ECHR cases related to the rulings of these judges amount to approximately 1.4 million złoty (42,700 zł for Demendecki, 42,700 zł for Dobrowolski, 512,400 zł for Motuk, 580,720 zł for Siwek, 224,175 zł for Szczepaniec, and 96,075 zł for Wojciechowski). With around 1,000 similar cases pending, continued service by these judges may impose further financial burdens on the state and taxpayers. The PG also criticized the process, noting that the list of candidates was submitted by a person with questionable appointment credentials and close ties to the executive branch, including President Andrzej Duda, who made the final appointments. I prezes SN Małgorzata Manowska acknowledged the PG’s submission, stating that the decision on response rests with President Kozielewicz, but affirmed that the Supreme Court will continue to uphold judicial integrity and combat such issues. The Izba Odpowiedzialności Zawodowej was established in July 2022 following amendments to the Supreme Court Act, replacing the former Izba Dyscyplinarna (Disciplinary Chamber).

<Summary source="https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/04/anthropic-ai-pentagon-defense-business-risk.html">
This summary focuses on the key facts, statistics, and expert opinions regarding Anthropic’s "supply chain risk" designation and its implications for the company’s standing as of early March 2026.

### **1. Facts, Statistics, and Objective Measurements**
*   **Financial Growth:** Anthropic’s annual revenue run rate is nearing **$20 billion** (up from $14 billion weeks prior). A recent $30 billion funding round valued the company at approximately **$380 billion**.
*   **Customer Base:** As of January 2026, over **80%** of Anthropic’s business came from enterprise customers.
*   **Market Performance:** On February 28, 2026 (the day after the dispute began), the Claude chatbot reached **#1** on Apple’s U.S. rankings for top free apps.
*   **Product Traction:** "Claude Code," a coding assistant, is cited as one of the company's fastest-growing products, generating billions in annualized revenue.
*   **Precedent:** The "supply chain risk" designation has historically been reserved for entities allegedly controlled by foreign adversaries (e.g., Russia or China).
*   **Legal Status:** Anthropic has called the designation "legally unsound" and intends to contest it in court. A tech industry trade group has also formally expressed concern to Secretary Hegseth.

### **2. Opinions from Reliable and Named Sources**
*   **Emil Michael (DoD Chief Technology Officer):** Stated that Anthropic "closed the door" by refusing terms. He argued Anthropic’s "constitution" and values are "not compatible" with the Department of War and emphasized that the government must avoid "single vendor concentration" (referencing a new contract awarded to OpenAI).
*   **Spencer Penn (CEO of Light Source):** Notes that the "supply chain risk" phrase is significant because foundation model choices are now viewed as "infrastructure decisions" involving reputational and geopolitical risks. He expects "quiet evaluation of alternative foundation model providers" by companies with dual-use exposure.
*   **Alexander Harstrick (Managing Partner, J2 Ventures):** Confirmed that defense contractors are already dropping Anthropic technology because they must be "very strict" in interpreting requirements for large defense contracts.
*   **Michael Murphy (Global AI Readiness Lead, Adaptovate):** Opined that Fortune 500 procurement teams move quickly on "perceived compliance risk," which may accelerate a shift toward avoiding reliance on a single AI provider.
*   **Hugh Johnston (CFO, Disney):** Indicated that while Disney currently uses OpenAI, they expect to move toward a "multiple models" approach rather than a single provider.
*   **Joshua Morley (Global Head of AI, Akkodis):** Highlighted that while there is power in an ecosystem, there is also significant "lock-in risk" for enterprises.

### **3. Potentially Useful Opinions from Less Reliable/Unspecified Sources**
*   **Legal Experts (Unnamed):** Many agree with Anthropic that the government’s attempt to limit commercial activities of private companies (beyond specific procurement scenarios) likely exceeds statutory authority.
*   **Internal Sources (Unnamed):** Sources provided the specific revenue run rate figures ($14B to $20B) and the valuation details ($380B) following the recent funding round.
*   **General Market Sentiment:** The article notes that while the dispute helped Anthropic’s brand with consumers, it has created "inevitable conversations in boardrooms" regarding the long-term viability of the platform for enterprise infrastructure.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2026/03/pentagon-designates-anthropic-a-supply-chain-risk-what-government-contractors-need-to-know">
This summary outlines the key facts, legal frameworks, and expert observations regarding the February 2026 executive actions against Anthropic as detailed in the provided article.

### 1. Facts and Statistics
*   **Contract History:** In July 2025, Anthropic and the Pentagon entered a contract making Claude the first frontier AI model approved for classified networks.
*   **The Dispute:** The conflict arose from Anthropic’s Acceptable Use Policy (AUP), which prohibited Claude’s use for:
    1.  Mass domestic surveillance of Americans.
    2.  Fully autonomous weapons systems (targeting without human intervention).
*   **The Deadline:** The Pentagon set a deadline of 5:01 p.m. on Friday, February 27, 2026, for Anthropic to remove these restrictions and allow use for "all lawful purposes."
*   **Executive Action:** Following Anthropic’s refusal, President Trump directed all federal agencies to cease using Anthropic technology.
*   **Wind-down Period:** A six-month transition period was granted for certain agencies, including the Department of War.
*   **Immediate Restrictions:** The General Services Administration (GSA) has already removed Anthropic from **USAi.gov**, the government’s AI testing platform.
*   **Secretary Hegseth’s Directive:** Hegseth declared that no military contractor "may conduct any commercial activity with Anthropic."

### 2. Legal Frameworks and Procedures
The article identifies two primary statutes the administration may use to formalize this exclusion:

**Federal Acquisition Supply Chain Security Act of 2018 (FASCSA) / 41 U.S.C. § 4713:**
*   **Process:** Requires a recommendation from the Federal Acquisition Security Council (FASC) based on a risk assessment.
*   **Scope:** Can prohibit contractors from using specific "covered articles" (cloud services, software, etc.) in their federal work.
*   **Contractor Obligations:** Under FAR 52.204-30, contractors must check SAM.gov every three months, conduct "reasonable inquiries" into their supply chains, and report Anthropic use to contracting officers within three business days of a new order.
*   **Limitation:** FASCSA orders typically apply only to federal contract performance, not a contractor’s private commercial activity.

**10 U.S.C. § 3252:**
*   **Authority:** Specific to the Department of Defense (DoD). It allows the Secretary of Defense to exclude a source from procurements involving "national security systems."
*   **Requirements:** The Secretary must provide a written determination that the action is necessary for national security and that "less intrusive measures" are unavailable.
*   **Oversight:** Requires notice to congressional committees including a summary of the risk assessment.

### 3. Expert Opinions and Analysis
*   **Reliability of the "Supply Chain Risk" Label:** The article notes that while Secretary Hegseth used the phrase "supply chain risk," the administration has not yet publicly identified the specific legal authority (FASCSA vs. 10 U.S.C. § 3252) being invoked.
*   **Scope of the Ban:** Legal observers (the authors of the update) note a discrepancy between Trump’s rhetoric and existing law. While the President suggested a total ban on "any commercial activity" with Anthropic, the FASCSA framework is generally limited to a contractor's federal work and does not preclude private commercial use.
*   **Potential for Litigation:** The article characterizes the situation as "fluid," noting it could result in significant litigation or a negotiated settlement, especially given the procedural requirements (like notice and risk assessments) mandated by the statutes.
*   **Shift in Strategy:** The authors observe that the administration appears to have moved away from earlier threats to use the **Defense Production Act** (to force compliance) in favor of removing Anthropic from the supply chain entirely.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.businessinsider.com/sam-altman-openai-pentagon-government-deal-controversy-explained-2026-3">
The following summary extracts the key facts, statistics, and named opinions from the provided text regarding the potential designation of Anthropic as a supply chain risk.

### 1. Facts, Statistics, and Objective Measurements
*   **The Ultimatum:** Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth delivered an ultimatum to Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei to agree to the government's terms by **5:01 p.m. ET on February 27, 2026**, or face being blacklisted.
*   **Presidential Directive:** President Donald Trump directed every federal agency to "immediately cease" all use of Anthropic technology, citing a six-month phase-out period for certain agencies like the Department of War.
*   **Treasury Department Action:** Following the President's directive, the U.S. Treasury Department officially announced the termination of all Anthropic products, including the Claude platform.
*   **OpenAI Agreement:** OpenAI reached a deal with the Department of Defense (DOD) to deploy its models on the military's classified network. The contract is based on the standard of "all lawful use."
*   **Anthropic’s Stated Constraints:** Anthropic refused the DOD's terms on two specific grounds:
    1.  Refusal to provide AI to guide **fully autonomous weapons**.
    2.  Refusal to allow AI to be used for **mass domestic surveillance**, even if such searches might not technically violate current law.
*   **Consumer Response:** Following the controversy, some users defected from ChatGPT to Claude; notably, pop star Katy Perry publicly shared her subscription to the $214.99 annual Claude Pro plan.

### 2. Opinions from Reliable and Named Sources
*   **Donald Trump (President of the United States):** Labeled Anthropic a "radical left, woke company" and "leftwing nut jobs." He claimed they attempted to "strong-arm" the Department of War by forcing them to obey Terms of Service over the Constitution.
*   **Pete Hegseth (Secretary of Defense/War):** Stated his intention to formally designate Anthropic a "supply chain risk to national security." He asserted this would ban any company with DOD contracts from conducting commercial activity with Anthropic.
*   **Dario Amodei (CEO of Anthropic):** Stated the company "cannot in good conscience accede" to the Pentagon's requests regarding autonomous weapons and surveillance. He characterized the proposed supply chain designation as "unprecedented" and vowed to challenge it in court.
*   **Emil Michael (Pentagon R&D Official):** Accused Amodei of having a "God-complex" and attempting to "personally control the US Military."
*   **Sam Altman (CEO of OpenAI):** Acknowledged that the optics of OpenAI’s deal "don't look good" and were "rushed," but claimed the move was intended to "de-escalate" tensions between the industry and the government. He stated he does *not* support labeling Anthropic a supply chain risk.
*   **Miles Brundage (Former OpenAI Head of Policy):** Suggested that OpenAI "caved" to government pressure and "screwed Anthropic" while framing the deal as a safety-conscious partnership.

### 3. Potentially Useful Opinions from Less Reliable/Unnamed Sources
*   **Unnamed Defense Department Official:** Confirmed to *Business Insider* the specific timing and nature of Hegseth’s "blacklist" ultimatum to Amodei.
*   **Unnamed Source "Familiar with the Situation":** Informed *Business Insider* that OpenAI began its specific talks with the Pentagon on the Wednesday prior to the Friday deadline (February 25, 2026).
*   **Legal Observers/External Lawyers (Referenced):** The article notes that external lawyers and critics suggest the "all lawful use" clause in the OpenAI contract leaves the door open for the very surveillance and autonomous use cases Anthropic rejected.
</Summary>
